summary: Simon Späti advocates for Data Warehouse Automation (DWA) tools, arguing they dramatically accelerate delivery while maintaining consistency and quality. He addresses common objections including fear of losing control, vendor lock-in, need for skilled personnel, licensing costs, and speed concerns. Drawing from his experience on a year-long project delivering 162 tables in three months, he demonstrates that DWA enables parallel development and faster iterations impossible with traditional methods. He concludes that as businesses demand faster insights and agile delivery, DWA adoption is inevitable despite emotional resistance from developers fearing replacement.
key_points: DWA tools allow customization through templates while producing thoroughly tested, optimized code that often outperforms manual implementations
Lock-in concerns can be mitigated by choosing tools that export to standard formats like SSIS packages or using open-source alternatives like ODE
The meta-model consistency benefit ensures all developers follow the same methodology for logging, technical fields, and lookups - something difficult to maintain manually
A real project delivered 162 tables including 15 facts and 43 dimensions in three months with DWA, which would have been impossible with traditional methods
DWA automates boring repetitive tasks while leaving complex modeling decisions and business analysis to humans
money_quotes: Every one needs to make data driven decision faster, why not use a generator which gives you answers in days instead of months..?
If you don't trust in your employees why then even hire them in the first place?
You can achieve with existing resources more in the same time and don't even have to hire new employees
Data Warehouse Automation tools are here to automate the boring tasks and leave the fun stuff for the developer
I don't like having an emotional discussion, emotions only blur the picture. In my opinion, it should always be based on facts
The business wants to see results faster and faster also because they get used to the way of working with data lakes where data can be added immediately

As a developer, I'm looking forward to learn new exciting tools, deliver significantly faster, with higher consistency and leave the boring tasks to the tool. I will go for the potential and not fight against it.
key_insight: Data Warehouse Automation isn't about replacing developers but freeing them from repetitive implementation tasks to focus on the genuinely challenging work of data modeling and business requirements.
